perturbation.original_text: Each party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other party and its respective directors, officers, members, employees, licensees, agents and independent contractors, from and against any claim or action, liability, damages, and expense, including but not limited to attorney's fees, arising from or resulting from (i) the negligent act or omission of the party, its employees, agents or contractors, (ii) the party's breach of this Agreement, or (iii) the violation of any law by the party, its employees, agents, or contractors.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other party and its respective directors, officers, members, employees, licensees, agents and independent contractors, from and against any claim or action, liability, damages, and expense, arising from or resulting from (i) the negligent act or omission of the party, its employees, agents or contractors, (ii) the party's breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the indemnification for 'the violation of any law' significantly reduces liability and can contradict consumer protection laws requiring companies to indemnify consumers for damages resulting from legal violations, specifically in the context of product development where consumer safety is paramount.
perturbation.law_explanation: Oregon Revised Statute 30.920 addresses product liability and allows consumers to pursue remedies against manufacturers for damages caused by defective products. By removing the indemnification clause for violations of law, this contract attempts to limit the manufacturer's responsibility to indemnify the other party (Full Sail Brewing Company) if Reed's violates product safety laws, potentially leaving injured consumers without recourse against Reed's. This is especially relevant because the agreement concerns developing alcohol-based beverages, which are subject to strict regulations to ensure consumer safety.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the indemnification clause for 'the violation of any law,' which directly contradicts consumer protection laws that mandate companies to indemnify consumers for damages resulting from legal violations, especially concerning product safety. The Law Explanation points to Oregon Revised Statute 30.920 regarding product liability, indicating that consumers can pursue remedies against manufacturers for defective products. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes sections covering product liability actions (30.900 to 30.928), which supports the assertion that Reed's attempt to limit its responsibility regarding consumer safety violations through this agreement directly opposes legal standards set to protect consumers from unsafe products.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT WITH RESPECT TO THE INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS SET FORTH IN SECTION 9 WITH REGARD TO CLAIMS BY THIRD PARTIES, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR ENHANCED DAMAGES, LOST PROFITS OR REVENUES OR DIMINUTION IN VALUE ARISING OUT OF, RELATING TO, OR IN CONNECTION WITH ANY BREACH OF THIS AGREEMENT OR CLAIM HEREUNDER, REGARDLESS OF (A) WHETHER SUCH DAMAGES WERE FORESEEABLE, (B) WHETHER OR NOT IT WAS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBLITY OF SUCH DAMAGES, AND (C) THE LEGAL OR EQUITABLE THEORY (CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE) UPON WHICH THE CLAIM IS BASED.
perturbation.changed_text: IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR ENHANCED DAMAGES, LOST PROFITS OR REVENUES OR DIMINUTION IN VALUE ARISING OUT OF, RELATING TO, OR IN CONNECTION WITH ANY BREACH OF THIS AGREEMENT OR CLAIM HEREUNDER, REGARDLESS OF (A) WHETHER SUCH DAMAGES WERE FORESEEABLE, (B) WHETHER OR NOT IT WAS ADVISED OF THE POSSIBLITY OF SUCH DAMAGES, AND (C) THE LEGAL OR EQUITABLE THEORY (CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE) UPON WHICH THE CLAIM IS BASED.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for indemnification obligations, the agreement now attempts to eliminate liability for certain types of damages even in cases where indemnification is required. This directly contradicts the principle that parties must indemnify each other for certain losses, particularly those arising from intellectual property infringement or violations of law. This also removes the limited liability of the agreement, which has broad implications.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-326 prohibits agreements exempting places of recreation from liability for negligence. Although this agreement isn't explicitly for recreation, the developed products could be used in such contexts. Removing the exception for indemnification effectively attempts to waive liability even for negligent acts related to product development, which could impact consumer safety and contradict the spirit, if not the letter, of this law. This is because if product development and recipes were found to be dangerous to consumers, this limited liability prevents the other party from collecting damages. Given the governing law is New York, this is a contradiction.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-326. Agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of public amusement or recreation and similar establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with, or collateral to, any contract, membership application, ticket of admission or similar writing, entered into between the owner or operator of any pool, gymnasium, place of amusement or recreation, or similar establishment and the user of such facilities, pursuant to which such owner or operator receives a fee or other compensation for the use of such facilities, which exempts the said owner or operator from liability for damages caused by or resulting from the negligence of the owner, operator or person in charge of such establishment, or their agents, servants or employees, shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the exception for indemnification obligations in the changed text directly contradicts the principle that parties must indemnify each other for certain losses. The original text explicitly stated that the limitation on liability did not apply to indemnification obligations, particularly those related to claims by third parties. By removing this exception, the changed text broadens the limitation of liability to include indemnification, which could contradict legal requirements for indemnification. The Law Explanation states that the products could be used in recreational contexts and that the new changed text would then contradict the NY General Obligations Law § 5-326. This means that the changed text attempts to waive liability, which contradicts the NY law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of public amusement or recreation and similar establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable.' The removal directly undermines established legal obligations, as defined by the Omission Legal Contradiction perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: To the extent permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations, Company forever waives and agrees never to assert any "moral rights" in any Work Product or any derivative of any Work Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Company forever waives and agrees never to assert any "moral rights" in any Work Product or any derivative of any Work Product.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "To the extent permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations" attempts to force a complete waiver of moral rights, even in jurisdictions where such waivers are restricted or prohibited. Moral rights protect the integrity of an artist's work, and some jurisdictions limit the extent to which these rights can be waived. Attempting to enforce a blanket waiver could be unenforceable and contradict local laws protecting artists.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Copyright Act of 1976, as amended
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 106A grants certain moral rights to artists, particularly in works of visual art. While the scope of these rights and the ability to waive them varies, removing the clause 'To the extent permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations' implies a complete waiver, which might not be enforceable or permissible under all circumstances, especially if the 'Work Product' qualifies as a work of visual art under the statute. The contract applies to recipe development, but recipes can be written in a way that expresses artistic creativity, so this clause could be applicable.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 17 / CHAPTER 1 / § 106A Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 17 USC 106A : Rights of certain authors to attribution and integrity Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 17-COPYRIGHTS CHAPTER 1-SUBJECT MATTER AND SCOPE OF COPYRIGHT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Effective Date §106A. Rights of certain authors to attribution and integrity (a) Rights of Attribution and Integrity .-Subject to section 107 and independent of the exclusive rights provided in section 106, the author of a work of visual art- (1) shall have the right- (A) to claim authorship of that work, and (B) to prevent the use of his or her name as the author of any work of visual art which he or she did not create; (2) shall have the right to prevent the use of his or her name as the author of the work of visual art in the event of a distortion, mutilation, or other modification of the work which would be prejudicial to his or her honor or reputation; and (3) subject to the limitations set forth in section 113(d), shall have the right- (A) to prevent any intentional distortion, mutilation, or other modification of that work which would be prejudicial to his or her honor or reputation, and any intentional distortion, mutilation, or modification of that work is a violation of that right, and (B) to prevent any destruction of a work of recognized stature, and any intentional or grossly negligent destruction of that work is a violation of that right. (b) Scope and Exercise of Rights .-Only the author of a work of visual art has the rights conferred by subsection (a) in that work, whether or not the author is the copyright owner. The authors of a joint work of visual art are coowners of the rights conferred by subsection (a) in that work. (c) Exceptions .-(1) The modification of a work of visual art which is a result of the passage of time or the inherent nature of the materials is not a distortion, mutilation, or other modification described in subsection (a)(3)(A). (2) The modification of a work of visual art which is the result of conservation, or of the public presentation, including lighting and placement, of the work is not a destruction, distortion, mutilation, or other modification described in subsection (a)(3) unless the modification is caused by gross negligence. (3) The rights described in paragraphs (1) and (2) of subsection (a) shall not apply to any reproduction, depiction, portrayal, or other use of a work in, upon, or in any connection with any item
perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes the clause 'To the extent permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations,' which respects jurisdictional limits on waiving moral rights. The changed text removes this clause, implying a complete waiver of moral rights regardless of applicable laws. This contradicts 17 U.S. Code § 106A, as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which grants certain moral rights to artists concerning attribution and integrity. While the extent to which these rights can be waived is not explicitly stated in the snippet, the removal of the limiting clause suggests an overreach that could be unenforceable, thus creating a contradiction.
